Meiji | predecessor7 = Position established
[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] (as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}) | successor7 = {{ill|Kuroda Kiyotaka|en|Kuroda Kiyotaka|qid=Q714049|lt=Kuroda Kiyotaka|1=Kuroda Kiyotaka}}{{Collapsed infobox section begin|Additional positions}} | office8 = [[:en:President of the House of Peers|President of the House of Peers]] | term_start8 = 24 October 1890 | term_end8 = 20 July 1891 | predecessor8 = Position established | successor8 = {{ill|Hachisuka Mochiaki|en|Hachisuka Mochiaki|qid=Q656008|lt=Hachisuka Mochiaki|1=Hachisuka Mochiaki}} | office9 = {{ill|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q1355043|lt=Minister for Foreign Affairs|1=Minister for Foreign Affairs (Japan)}} of Japan | term_start9 = September 1887 | term_end9 = February 1888 | primeminister9 = Himself | predecessor9 = {{ill|Inoue Kaoru|en|Inoue Kaoru|qid=Q493818|lt=Inoue Kaoru|1=Inoue Kaoru}} | successor9 = Ōkuma Shigenobu {{Collapsed infobox section end}} | pronunciation = | birth_name = Hayashi Risuke | birth_date = {{birth date|1841|10|16|df=y}} | birth_place = {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|qid=Q823502|lt=Tsukari|1=Hikari, Yamaguchi}}, Suō, Tokugawa shogunate (present-day {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Japan) | death_date = {{death date and age|1909|10|26|1841|10|16|df=y}} | death_place = {{ill|Harbin|en|Harbin|qid=Q42956|lt=Harbin|1=Harbin}}, Heilongjiang, Qing dynasty | death_cause = [[#Assassination|Assassination by gunshot]] | resting_place = Hirobumi Itō Cemetery, Tokyo, Japan | resting_place_coordinates = | party = [[:en:Independent (politician)|Independent]] {{small|(Before 1900)}}
{{ill|Rikken Seiyūkai|en|Rikken Seiyūkai|qid=Q705368|lt=Constitutional Association of Political Friendship|1=Rikken Seiyūkai}} {{small|(1900–1909)}} | spouse = Itō Umeko (1848–1924) | partner = | relations = | children = 3 sons, 2 daughters | alma_mater = {{ill|University College London|en|University College London|qid=Q193196|lt=University College London|1=University College London}}{{cite web|url= https://www.ucl.ac.uk/about/who/history/famous-alumni|title=Famous Alumni|work=UCL|date=11 January 2018}} | parents = | mother = | father = {{ill|Itō Jūzō|en|Itō Jūzō|qid=Q110982504|lt=Itō Jūzō|1=Itō Jūzō}} | signature = Ito Hirobumi Kao.png }} [[Kazoku|Prince]] {{nihongo|'''Itō Hirobumi'''|伊藤 博文|4=16 October 1841 – 26 October 1909}} was a Japanese politician who served as the first {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister of Japan|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} from 1885 to 1888, and later from 1892 to 1896, in 1898, and from 1900 to 1901. He was a leading member of the ''{{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|qid=Q254907|lt=Genrō|1=Genrō}}'', a group of senior statesmen that dictated policy during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. Even out of office as {{ill|Head of government|en|Head of government|qid=Q2285706|lt=Head of government|1=Head of government}}, Itō continued to wield vast influence over Japan's policies as a permanent imperial adviser (''genkun'') and frequent president of the emperor's {{ill|Privy Council of Japan|en|Privy Council of Japan|qid=Q702365|lt=Privy Council|1=Privy Council of Japan}}. A staunch {{ill|Monarchism|en|Monarchism|qid=Q216669|lt=monarchist|1=Monarchism}} and leading proponent of Japan's {{ill|Westernization|en|Westernization|qid=Q6498273|lt=Westernization|1=Westernization}}, Itō favored a large, all-powerful {{ill|Bureaucracy|en|Bureaucracy|qid=Q72468|lt=Bureaucracy|1=Bureaucracy}} that answered solely to the emperor, and opposed the formation of [[:en:political parties|political parties]]. Born into a poor farming family in the {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}}, Itō and his father were adopted into a low-Ranking {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} family. He joined the nationalist ''[[sonnō jōi]]'' movement after the {{ill|Opening of Japan|en|Opening of Japan|qid=Q11655196|lt=Opening of Japan|1=Opening of Japan}} in 1854, and in 1863 was sent to England to study at {{ill|University College London|en|University College London|qid=Q193196|lt=University College London|1=University College London}}. After the {{ill|Shimonoseki campaign|en|Shimonoseki campaign|qid=Q891773|lt=Shimonoseki campaign|1=Shimonoseki campaign}} of 1864, in which four Western powers bombed Chōshū, he resolved to set Japan on a path of Westernization. After the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868, Itō was appointed the junior councilor for foreign affairs in the new {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}. In 1870, he traveled to the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} to study Western currency, and in 1871 helped establish Japan's taxation system. He then set off on another overseas trip with the {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}} to the U.S. and Europe, and upon his return to Japan in 1873, became a full councilor and public works minister. Itō also served as home minister from 1878, and by 1881 he had become the ''{{ill|De facto|en|De facto|qid=Q712144|lt=De facto|1=De facto}}'' leader of the {{ill|Meiji oligarchy|en|Meiji oligarchy|qid=Q1150742|lt=Meiji oligarchy|1=Meiji oligarchy}}. After the advent of the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|en|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|qid=Q711511|lt=Freedom and People's Rights Movement|1=Freedom and People's Rights Movement}} and political parties, in 1881 Itō promised a constitution and national assembly. In 1882, he embarked on a trip to Europe to study its constitutions; his preference was for {{ill|Constitution of Prussia (1850)|en|Constitution of Prussia (1850)|qid=Q667025|lt=a Prussian-style one|1=Constitution of Prussia (1850)}} which would retain considerable power for the emperor and limit party involvement in the cabinet. He replaced religious references with those rooted in the Japanese concept of ''[[kokutai]]'' ("national polity"), which became the constitutional justification for imperial authority. In 1884, Itō reorganized [[Kazoku|the peerage]] to fill the seats in the anticipated {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|qid=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. In 1885, he replaced the ''[[Daijō-kan]]'' with {{ill|Cabinet of Japan|en|Cabinet of Japan|qid=Q529012|lt=a cabinet|1=Cabinet of Japan}} composed of ministry heads, and himself took up the new position of prime minister. When the draft constitution was ready in 1888, Itō established a supra-cabinet {{ill|Privy Council of Japan|en|Privy Council of Japan|qid=Q702365|lt=Privy Council|1=Privy Council of Japan}} to discuss and approve it on the emperor's behalf. He resigned as prime minister so he could head this new body. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} was proclaimed in 1889, and the [[:en:Imperial Diet (Japan)|Imperial Diet]] was first assembled in 1890. From 1892 to 1896, Itō was again prime minister. During his term, Japan defeated China in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} of 1894–1895, and he was involved in the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|qid=Q269655|lt=Treaty of Shimonoseki|1=Treaty of Shimonoseki}}, which annexed {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|qid=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}} to the empire and freed Korea from the {{ill|Tributary system of China|en|Tributary system of China|qid=Q6006438|lt=Chinese tributary system|1=Tributary system of China}}. During Itō’s third term as prime minister in 1898, his tax policies were opposed by political parties and he soon resigned. In 1900, he started his fourth term and formed his own pro-government party, the {{ill|Rikken Seiyūkai|en|Rikken Seiyūkai|qid=Q705368|lt=Rikken Seiyūkai|1=Rikken Seiyūkai}}, but continued to face opposition, and resigned in 1901. After Japan's victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}} of 1904–1905, Itō became the {{ill|Japanese Resident-General of Korea|en|Japanese Resident-General of Korea|qid=Q24856863|lt=resident-general of Korea|1=Japanese Resident-General of Korea}}, a nation which Japan desired to annex. Korea was declared a Japanese protectorate in 1905, and in 1907 Itō obliged {{ill|Gojong of Korea|en|Gojong of Korea|qid=Q257425|lt=its emperor|1=Gojong of Korea}} to step down. Itō was made a prince that year and resigned as resident-general in 1909; later that year, he was assassinated while in {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|qid=Q81126|lt=Manchuria|1=Manchuria}} by {{ill|An Jung-geun|en|An Jung-geun|qid=Q482413|lt=a Korean nationalist|1=An Jung-geun}}. == Biography == === Early years === [[File:Itō Hirobumi 1863.jpg|thumb|left|145px|Itō Hirobumi as a samurai in his youth]] Hayashi Risuke ({{lang|ja|林利助}}) was born on 16 October 1841, in Tsukari, {{ill|Kumage District, Yamaguchi|en|Kumage District, Yamaguchi|qid=Q1042808|lt=Kumage|1=Kumage District, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|qid=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province|1=Suō Province}} (present-day {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|qid=Q823502|lt=Hikari|1=Hikari, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}), the eldest son of farmer {{ill|Itō Jūzō|en|Itō Jūzō|qid=Q110982504|lt=Hayashi Jūzō|1=Itō Jūzō}} and his wife Kotoko. After his father went bankrupt and left for {{ill|Hagi, Yamaguchi|en|Hagi, Yamaguchi|qid=Q378225|lt=Hagi, Yamaguchi|1=Hagi, Yamaguchi}} in 1846, he went to live at his mother's parental home. In 1849, Jūzō invited the family to Hagi and the family reunited. There Risuke entered Kubo Gorō Saemon's school. Because the family was poor, when Risuke was 12, Jūzō was adopted by samurai servant Mizui Buhē. In 1854, Mizui Buhē was adopted by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} foot soldier (''{{ill|Ashigaru|en|Ashigaru|qid=Q725340|lt=Ashigaru|1=Ashigaru}}'') Itō Yaemon from Aihata, {{ill|Saba District, Yamaguchi|en|Saba District, Yamaguchi|qid=Q7395781|lt=Saba|1=Saba District, Yamaguchi}}. Mizui Buhē was renamed Itō Naoemon, Jūzō took the name Itō Jūzō, and Hayashi Risuke was renamed Itō Shunsuke at first, then Itō Hirobumi. These adoptions allowed both Hirobumi and his father Jūzō to rise to the samurai class and become ''ashigaru''.{{Cite book |last1=Itō |first1=Yukio |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/466068077 |title=Itō Hirobumi : kindai Nihon o tsukutta otoko |last2=伊藤之雄 |date=2009 |publisher=Kōdansha |others=之雄 伊藤 |isbn=978-4-06-215909-8 |pages=22–25 |oclc=466068077}} Jūzō was the biological son of {{Nihongo|Hayashi Sukezaemon|林助左衛門}}, a 5th generation descendant of {{Nihongo|Hayashi Nobuyoshi|林信吉}} who was a member of the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Hayashi clan (Owari)|en|Hayashi clan (Owari)|qid=Q2975264|lt=Hayashi clan of Owari|1=Hayashi clan (Owari)}}|尾張林氏}}. [[File:Choshu Five (1863).jpg|thumb|right|250px|Clockwise from top left: {{ill|Endō Kinsuke|en|Endō Kinsuke|qid=Q704720|lt=Endō Kinsuke|1=Endō Kinsuke}}, [[:en:Inoue Masaru (bureaucrat)|Nomura Yakichi]], [[Itō Hirobumi|Itō Shunsuke]], {{ill|Yamao Yōzō|en|Yamao Yōzō|qid=Q704979|lt=Yamao Yōzō|1=Yamao Yōzō}}, and {{ill|Inoue Kaoru|en|Inoue Kaoru|qid=Q493818|lt=Inoue Monta|1=Inoue Kaoru}}, photographed in 1863]] He was a student of {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|en|Yoshida Shōin|qid=Q559747|lt=Yoshida Shōin|1=Yoshida Shōin}} at the [[:en:Shōka Sonjuku|Shōka Sonjuku]] and later joined the ''[[Sonnō jōi]]'' movement ("to revere the Emperor and expel the barbarians"), together with [[:en:Katsura Kogorō|Katsura Kogorō]]. Active in the movement, he took part in an incendiary attack of the British legation on 31 January 1863 led by {{ill|Takasugi Shinsaku|en|Takasugi Shinsaku|qid=Q700310|lt=Takasugi Shinsaku|1=Takasugi Shinsaku}}, and in the company of {{ill|Yamao Yōzō|en|Yamao Yōzō|qid=Q704979|lt=Yamao Yōzō|1=Yamao Yōzō}} attacked and mortally wounded the head of the {{ill|Wagakukōdansho|en|Wagakukōdansho|qid=Q85877959|lt=Wagakukōdansho|1=Wagakukōdansho}} institute on 2 February 1863, believing a false report that the institute was looking into ways of toppling the Emperor. {{Cite book | last = Takii | first = Kazuhiro | title = Itō Hirobumi - Japan's First Prime Minister and Father of the Meiji Constitution | publisher = Routledge | others = trans. Takeshi Manabu | year = 2014 | isbn = 978-0415838863 }} Itō was chosen as one of the {{ill|Chōshū Five|en|Chōshū Five|qid=Q1071683|lt=Chōshū Five|1=Chōshū Five}} who studied at {{ill|University College London|en|University College London|qid=Q193196|lt=University College London|1=University College London}} in 1863, and the experience in {{ill|United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland|en|United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland|qid=Q174193|lt=Great Britain|1=United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland}} eventually convinced him Japan needed to adopt Western ways. In 1864, Itō returned to Japan with fellow student {{ill|Inoue Kaoru|en|Inoue Kaoru|qid=Q493818|lt=Inoue Kaoru|1=Inoue Kaoru}} to attempt to warn Chōshū Domain against going to war with the foreign powers (the [[:en:Bombardment of Shimonoseki|Bombardment of Shimonoseki]]) over the right of passage through the [[:en:Straits of Shimonoseki|Straits of Shimonoseki]]. At that time, he met [[:en:Ernest Satow|Ernest Satow]] for the first time, later a lifelong friend. === Political career === ==== Rise to power ==== [[File:Iwakura mission.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Photo of Itō (second from right, standing) alongside other members of the {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}}]] After the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868, Itō was appointed governor of {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, junior councilor for Foreign Affairs, and sent to the United States in 1870 to study Western currency systems. Returning to Japan in 1871, he established Japan's taxation system. With the advice of [[:en:Edmund Morel (railway engineer)|Edmund Morel]], a chief engineer of the railway department, Itō endeavored to found the {{ill|Ministry of Public Works (Japan)|en|Ministry of Public Works (Japan)|qid=Q65063828|lt=Public Works|1=Ministry of Public Works (Japan)}} together with [[:en:Yamao Yozo|Yamao Yozo]]. Later that year, he was sent on the {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}} around the world as vice-envoy extraordinary, during which he won the confidence of {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo Toshimichi|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}}, one of the leaders of the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}}.{{cite web |title=Itō Hirobumi |url=https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ito-Hirobumi |publisher=Britannica |access-date=28 April 2021}} In 1873, Itō was made a full councilor, Minister of Public Works, and in 1875 chairman of the first Assembly of Prefectural Governors. He participated in the {{ill|Osaka Conference of 1875|en|Osaka Conference of 1875|qid=Q2916939|lt=Osaka Conference of 1875|1=Osaka Conference of 1875}}. After Ōkubo's assassination, he took over the post of [[:en:Home Ministry (Japan)|Home Minister]] and secured a central position in the Meiji government. By 1881, he successfully pushed for the resignation of {{ill|Ōkuma Shigenobu|en|Ōkuma Shigenobu|qid=Q243141|lt=Ōkuma Shigenobu|1=Ōkuma Shigenobu}}, thereby allowing him to emerge as the ''{{ill|De facto|en|De facto|qid=Q712144|lt=De facto|1=De facto}}'' leader of the Meiji government.{{cite encyclopedia |last=Perez |first=Louis G. |editor1-last= Perez |editor1-first= Louis G. |title=Itō Hirobumi |encyclopedia=Japan at War:An Encyclopedia |publisher=ABC-CLIO, LLC |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&dq=Ito+assumed+primary+leadership&pg=PA149 |isbn = 9781598847420 |access-date=11 September 2022 |page=149 |date = 8 January 2013}}{{cite encyclopedia |last=Grunden |first=Walter E. |editor1-last= Perez |editor1-first= Louis G. |title=Ōkuma Shigenobu |encyclopedia=Japan at War:An Encyclopedia |publisher=ABC-CLIO, LLC |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vl7Auu2UVEsC&dq=Okuma+forced+out&pg=PA295 |isbn = 9781598847420 |access-date=16 April 2023 |page=295 |date = 8 January 2013 |quote=In 1878, Ōkuma was placed in charge of the bureau for land tax revision, where he attempted to enforce a series of unsuccessful programs geared toward financial retrenchment. Despite economic setbacks, his public popularity grew because he favored the immediate adoption of a British-style constitution and parliamentary government. Consequently, Ōkuma found himself the chief political rival and competitor of Itō Hirobumi, who championed the Prussian-style constitutional monarchy. In 1881, as the popular rights movement was gaining momentum, Ōkuma publicly advocated the immediate establishment of a national assembly. That stand placed him in direct opposition to Itō, and as a result, he was forced out of office in 1881.}} Itō went to Europe in 1882 to study the constitutions of those countries, spending nearly 18 months away from Japan. While working on a constitution for Japan, he also wrote the first {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}} and established the Japanese {{ill|Peerage|en|Peerage|qid=Q51280089|lt=Peerage|1=Peerage}} system (''[[kazoku]]'') in 1884. In 1885, he negotiated the {{ill|Convention of Tientsin|en|Convention of Tientsin|qid=Q705189|lt=Convention of Tientsin|1=Convention of Tientsin}} with {{ill|Li Hongzhang|en|Li Hongzhang|qid=Q378517|lt=Li Hongzhang|1=Li Hongzhang}}, normalizing Japan's diplomatic relations with {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing-dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}} China. In the same year, In 1885, Itō established a [[:en:cabinet system|cabinet system]] of government based on European ideas, replacing the [[Daijō-kan]] as the nation's main policy-making organization. ==== As Prime Minister ==== [[File:Prime Minister Hirobumi Ito.jpg|thumb|left|upright|240px|Itō Hirobumi as prime minister (c. 1880s)]] On 22 December 1885, Itō became the first {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister of Japan|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}, as the head of {{ill|First Itō Cabinet|en|First Itō Cabinet|qid=Q1045014|lt=First Itō Cabinet|1=First Itō cabinet}}. The first Itō Cabinet endeavored to establish institutions preparatory to the promulgation of the Constitution, and in February 1886 established a system of government for each ministry, and in March, the Imperial University (now the University of Tokyo) was established, and in March of the following year, a national academic association was established and supported it. On the other hand, Inoue Kaoru was appointed Minister of Foreign Affairs and was given responsibility for amending the treaty, but the amendment proposed by Inoue included the appointment of foreign judges, leading to the problem of appointing private officials. foreign legislation, leading to In July 1887, a revision meeting aimed at foreign countries was cancelled, and Inoue Kaoru resigned in September, leading to defeat. In June of the same year, he began studying the draft constitution with Itō Miyoji, Inoue Tsuyoshi, Kaneko Kentaro and others in Tsushima. On 30 April 1888, Itō resigned as prime minister, but headed the new [[:en:Privy Council (Japan)|Privy Council]] to maintain power behind-the-scenes. In 1889, he also became the first ''{{ill|Genrō|en|Genrō|qid=Q254907|lt=Genrō|1=Genrō}}''. The {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} was promulgated in February 1889. He had added to it the references to the ''[[kokutai]]'' or "national polity" as the justification of the emperor's authority through his divine descent and the unbroken line of emperors, and the unique relationship between subject and sovereign.W. G. Beasley,''The Rise of Modern Japan'', pp. 79–80 {{ISBN|0-312-04077-6}} This stemmed from his rejection of some European notions as unfit for Japan, as they stemmed from European constitutional practice and Christianity. He remained a powerful force while {{ill|Kuroda Kiyotaka|en|Kuroda Kiyotaka|qid=Q714049|lt=Kuroda Kiyotaka|1=Kuroda Kiyotaka}} and {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|qid=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo|1=Yamagata Aritomo}}, his political nemeses,{{According to whom|date=March 2021}} were prime ministers. During {{ill|Second Itō Cabinet|en|Second Itō Cabinet|qid=Q11600801|lt=Itō's second term as prime minister|1=Second Itō cabinet}} (8 August 1892 – 31 August 1896), he supported the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and negotiated the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|qid=Q269655|lt=Treaty of Shimonoseki|1=Treaty of Shimonoseki}} in March 1895, {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|qid=Q137816|lt=made Taiwan a Japanese colony|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}} with his ailing foreign minister {{ill|Mutsu Munemitsu|en|Mutsu Munemitsu|qid=Q704098|lt=Mutsu Munemitsu|1=Mutsu Munemitsu}}. In the {{ill|Anglo-Japanese Treaty of Commerce and Navigation|en|Anglo-Japanese Treaty of Commerce and Navigation|qid=Q2915092|lt=Anglo-Japanese Treaty of Commerce and Navigation|1=Anglo-Japanese Treaty of Commerce and Navigation}} of 1894, he succeeded in removing some of the onerous [[:en:Unequal treaty|Unequal treaty]] clauses that had plagued Japanese foreign relations since the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. During {{ill|Third Itō Cabinet|en|Third Itō Cabinet|qid=Q11601105|lt=Itō's third term as prime minister|1=Third Itō cabinet}} (12 January – 30 June 1898), he was forced to contend with the rise of {{ill|political party|en|political party|qid=Q7278|lt=political parties|1=Political party}}. Both the {{ill|Liberal Party (Japan, 1890)|en|Liberal Party (Japan, 1890)|qid=Q11612923|lt=Liberal Party|1=Liberal Party (Japan, 1890)}} and the ''{{ill|Shimpotō|en|Shimpotō|qid=Q3062249|lt=Shimpotō|1=Shimpotō}}'' opposed his proposed new land taxes, and in retaliation, Itō dissolved the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=lower house|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} of the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=Imperial Diet|1=National Diet}} and called for {{ill|March 1898 Japanese general election|en|March 1898 Japanese general election|qid=Q1195309|lt=general election|1=March 1898 Japanese general election}}. As a result, both parties merged into the ''{{ill|Kenseitō|en|Kenseitō|qid=Q2915065|lt=Kenseitō|1=Kenseitō}}'', won a majority of the seats, and forced Itō to resign. This lesson taught Itō the need for a pro-government political party, so he organized the ''{{ill|Rikken Seiyūkai|en|Rikken Seiyūkai|qid=Q705368|lt=Rikken Seiyūkai|1=Rikken Seiyūkai}}'' (Constitutional Association of Political Friendship) in 1900. Itō's womanizing was a popular theme in editorial cartoons and in parodies by contemporary comedians, and was used by his political enemies in their campaign against him.{{Citation needed|reason=This needs to be supported|date=September 2018}} Itō returned to office as {{ill|Fourth Itō Cabinet|en|Fourth Itō Cabinet|qid=Q11601307|lt=prime minister for a fourth term|1=Fourth Itō cabinet}} from 19 October 1900, to 10 May 1901, this time facing political opposition from the [[:en:Japanese House of Peers|House of Peers]]. Weary of political back-stabbing, he resigned in 1901, but remained as head of the Privy Council as the premiership alternated between {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|qid=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi|1=Saionji Kinmochi}} and {{ill|Katsura Tarō|en|Katsura Tarō|qid=Q159048|lt=Katsura Tarō|1=Katsura Tarō}}. [[File:Picture of Hirobumi Itō.jpg|thumb|upright|right|210px|Itō in the later years of his political career]] Toward the end of August 1901, Itō announced his intention of visiting the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} to recuperate. This turned into a long journey in the course of which he visited the major cities of the United States and {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|qid=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}. He set off from {{ill|Yokohama|en|Yokohama|qid=Q38283|lt=Yokohama|1=Yokohama}} on 18 September, traveled through the U.S. to {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|qid=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}}, and received an honorary doctorate ([[:en:LL.D.|LL.D.]]) from {{ill|Yale University|en|Yale University|qid=Q49112|lt=Yale University|1=Yale University}} in late October.{{Cite news |newspaper=The Times |title=United States |date=24 October 1901 |page=3 |issue=36594}} He then sailed to [[:en:Boulogne|Boulogne]], reaching Paris on 4 November. On 25 November, he reached {{ill|Saint Petersburg|en|Saint Petersburg|qid=Q656|lt=Saint Petersburg|1=Saint Petersburg}}, having been asked by the new prime minister, Katsura Tarō, to sound out the Russians, entirely unofficially, on their intentions in the Far East. Japan hoped to achieve what it called ''Man-Kan kōkan'', the exchange of a free hand for Russia in Manchuria for a free hand for Japan in Korea, but Russia, feeling greatly superior to Japan and unwilling to give up the use of Korean ports for its navy, was in no mood to compromise. Foreign minister {{ill|Vladimir Lamsdorf|en|Vladimir Lamsdorf|qid=Q142580|lt=Vladimir Lamsdorf|1=Vladimir Lamsdorf}} "thought that time was on the side of his country because of the [[Trans-Siberian Railway|[Trans-Siberian] railway]] and there was no need to make concessions to the Japanese".Ian Nish, ''The Origins of the Russo-Japanese War'' (Longman, 1985; {{ISBN|0582491142}}), p. 118. Itō left empty-handed for {{ill|Berlin|en|Berlin|qid=Q64|lt=Berlin|1=Berlin}} (where he received honors from [[:en:Wilhelm II, German Emperor|Kaiser Wilhelm]]), {{ill|Brussels|en|Brussels|qid=Q240|lt=Brussels|1=Brussels}}, and London. Meanwhile, Katsura had decided that ''Man-Kan kōkan'' was no longer desirable for Japan, which should not renounce activity in Manchuria.{{Citation needed|reason=This needs to be supported|date=November 2019}} In Britain, Itō met with {{ill|Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne|en|Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne|qid=Q337386|lt=Lord Lansdowne|1=Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 5th Marquess of Lansdowne}}, which helped lay the groundwork for the {{ill|Anglo-Japanese Alliance|en|Anglo-Japanese Alliance|qid=Q541195|lt=Anglo-Japanese Alliance|1=Anglo-Japanese Alliance}} announced early the following year. The failure of his mission to Russia was "one of the most important events in the run-up to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}".Nish, ''The Origins of the Russo-Japanese War'', p. 116. While Prime Minister, Itō invited Professor {{ill|George Trumbull Ladd|en|George Trumbull Ladd|qid=Q3328844|lt=George Trumbull Ladd|1=George Trumbull Ladd}} of {{ill|Yale University|en|Yale University|qid=Q49112|lt=Yale University|1=Yale University}} to serve as a diplomatic adviser to promote mutual understanding between Japan and the United States. Lectures delivered by Ladd in Japan revolutionized its educational methods; he was the first foreigner to receive the Third Class honor (conferred by the Emperor in 1899) and the Second Class honor (in 1907) in the [[:en:Orders of the Rising Sun|Orders of the Rising Sun]]. He later wrote a book on his personal experiences in Korea and with Resident-General Itō.[https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1908/02/22/105003345.pdf Topics of the Week: "George Trumbull Ladd"], ''The New York Times''. 22 February 1908.[https://web.archive.org/web/20071128152839/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,761252,00.html "Business: Japanese Strip"], ''Time''. 8 May 1939.[https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1899/10/22/117932119.pdf "American Honored by the Japanese"], ''The New York Times''. 22 October 1899. When Ladd died, half his ashes were buried in a Buddhist temple in Tokyo and a monument was erected to him.{{cite web | title = Great Head Temple Sôjiji | year = 2007 | url = http://www.terebess.hu/zen/sojiji/szodzsi.html | access-date=29 July 2009}} ==== As Resident-General of Korea ==== [[File:Prince Ito and Crown Prince of Korea.jpg|thumb|left|upright|220px|Prince Itō and the Crown Prince of Korea {{ill|Yi Un|en|Yi Un|qid=Q484866|lt=Yi Un|1=Yi Un}}]] On 9 November 1905, following the Russo-Japanese War, Itō arrived in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|qid=Q8684|lt=Hanseong|1=Seoul}} and gave a letter from the Emperor of Japan to [[:en:Gojong of the Korean Empire|Gojong]], Emperor of Korea, asking him to sign the {{ill|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905|en|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905|qid=Q233254|lt=Japan–Korea Protectorate Treaty|1=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905}}, which would make Korea a Japanese {{ill|protectorate|en|protectorate|qid=Q164142|lt=protectorate|1=Protectorate}}. On 15 November 1905, he ordered Japanese troops to encircle the {{ill|Gyeongbokgung|en|Gyeongbokgung|qid=Q482485|lt=Korean imperial palace|1=Gyeongbokgung}}. On 17 November 1905, Itō and Japanese Field Marshal {{ill|Hasegawa Yoshimichi|en|Hasegawa Yoshimichi|qid=Q1195299|lt=Hasegawa Yoshimichi|1=Hasegawa Yoshimichi}} entered the Jungmyeongjeon Hall, a Russian-designed building that was once part of [[:en:Deoksu Palace|Deoksu Palace]], to persuade Gojong to approve the treaty, but the Emperor refused. Itō then pressured the Emperor's ministers with the implied, and later stated, threat of bodily harm, to sign the treaty.{{Cite book |last=McKenzie |first=F.A. |title=Korea's Fight for Freedom |publisher=[[Fleming H. Revell Company]] |year=1920 |author-link=Frederick Arthur MacKenzie}} {{ill|Five Eulsa Traitors|en|Five Eulsa Traitors|qid=Q711646|lt=Five ministers|1=Five Eulsa Traitors}} signed an agreement that had been prepared by Itō in the Jungmyeongjeon. The agreement gave Imperial Japan complete responsibility for Korea's foreign affairs,United States. Dept. of State. (1919). {{Google books|35QpAAAAYAAJ|Catalogue of treaties: 1814–1918|page=273}} and placed all trade through Korean ports under Imperial Japanese supervision. After the treaty had been signed, Itō became the first [[:en:Resident-General of Korea|Resident-General of Korea]] on 21 December 1905. In 1907, he urged [[:en:Emperor Gojong|Emperor Gojong]] to abdicate in favor of his son [[:en:Sunjong|Sunjong]] and secured the {{ill|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1907|en|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1907|qid=Q713917|lt=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1907|1=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1907}}, thereby giving Japan authority to dictate Korea's internal affairs. While Itō was firmly against Korea falling into China or Russia's {{ill|Sphere of influence|en|Sphere of influence|qid=Q593682|lt=Sphere of influence|1=Sphere of influence}}, he also opposed its annexation, advocating instead that the territory should be treated as a protectorate. When the cabinet voted in favor of annexing Korea, he proposed that the process be delayed in the hopes that the decision could eventually be reversed.{{cite book|title=Hirobumi Itō and Korean Annexation (Itō hirobumi to kankoku heigou)|first=Fukuju|last=Umino|publisher=Aoki Shoten|year=2004|isbn= 978-4-250-20414-2|language=ja}} However, Itō ultimately changed his mind and approved plans to have the region annexed on 10 April 1909. Despite changing his position, he was forced to resign on 14 June 1909 by the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|qid=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}} (one of the foremost advocates for Korea's annexation).{{cite book|title=伊藤博文の韓国併合構想と朝鮮社会|first=Hiroyuki|last=Ogawara|publisher=岩波書店|year=2010|isbn=978-4000221795|language=ja}} His assassination is believed to have accelerated the path to the {{ill|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1910|en|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1910|qid=Q495558|lt=Japan–Korea Annexation Treaty|1=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1910}}.{{cite book|title=Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|first=Donald|last=Keene|author-link=Donald Keene|publisher=[[Columbia University Press]]|year=2002|pages=[https://archive.org/details/emperorofjapanme00keen/page/662 662–667]|isbn=0-231-12340-X|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/emperorofjapanme00keen/page/662}} == Assassination == [[File:Ito_before_death.jpg|thumb|Last image of Itō arriving at Harbin Station shortly before his assassination on 26 October 1909]] Itō arrived at the {{ill|Harbin railway station|en|Harbin railway station|qid=Q1059254|lt=Harbin railway station|1=Harbin railway station}} on 26 October 1909 for a meeting with {{ill|Vladimir Kokovtsov|en|Vladimir Kokovtsov|qid=Q438448|lt=Vladimir Kokovtsov|1=Vladimir Kokovtsov}}, a Russian representative in {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|qid=Q81126|lt=Manchuria|1=Manchuria}}. There {{ill|An Jung-geun|en|An Jung-geun|qid=Q482413|lt=An Jung-geun|1=An Jung-geun}}, a {{ill|Korean nationalism|en|Korean nationalism|qid=Q716341|lt=Korean nationalist|1=Korean nationalism}} and {{ill|Korean independence movement|en|Korean independence movement|qid=Q707483|lt=independence activist|1=Korean independence movement}},{{cite web|url=http://aboutjapan.japansociety.org/content.cfm/hero |title=What Defines a Hero? |publisher=Japan Society |access-date=29 January 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071004183049/http://aboutjapan.japansociety.org/content.cfm/hero |archive-date=4 October 2007}}{{Cite web|url=https://terms.naver.com/entry.nhn?cid=40942&docId=1122561&CategoryId=33384|title=안중근|website=terms.naver.com}} fired six shots, three of which hit Itō in the chest. He died shortly thereafter. His body was returned to Japan on the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|qid=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} cruiser {{ship|Japanese cruiser|Akitsushima||2}}, and he was accorded a {{ill|state funeral|en|state funeral|qid=Q1052001|lt=state funeral|1=State funeral}} on 4 November 1909 at Hibiya Park.{{cite book|last=Nakamura|first=Kaju|title=Prince Ito – The Man and Statesman – A Brief History of His Life|publisher=Lulu Press (reprint)|orig-year=1910|year=2010|isbn=978-1445571423}} An Jung-geun later listed "[[An Jung-geun#Assassination of Itō Hirobumi|15 reasons]] why Itō should be killed" at his trial.{{cite news |author= |title=The Harbin Tragedy |url= http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/Digitised/Article/straitstimes19091202-1.2.20?ST=1&AT=search&k=The%20Straits%20Times,%202%20December%201909|work= The Straits Times|date= 2 December 1909|access-date= 3 July 2017}}{{cite news |date= 24 August 2009|title= Why Did Ahn Jung-geun Kill Hirobumi Ito?|url= https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/news/nation/2009/09/192_50617.html|work={{ill|The Korea Times|en|The Korea Times|qid=Q486950|lt=The Korea Times|1=The Korea Times}}}} On 14 February 1910, Ahn was sentenced to death by hanging, Yu to two years in prison, and Cao and Liu to one year and six months in prison for murder and crimes against the Imperial Japanese Government. ==Legacy== {{Unbalanced|section|date=March 2015}} === In Japan === [[File:Series C 1K Yen Bank of Japan note - front.jpg|255px|thumb|A Series C 1,000 yen note of Japan, with a portrait of Itō Hirobumi]] * A portrait of Itō Hirobumi was on the obverse of the Series C {{ill|Banknotes of the Japanese yen|en|Banknotes of the Japanese yen|qid=Q839051|lt=1,000 yen note|1=Banknotes of the Japanese yen}} from 1963 until a new series was issued in 1984. * The publishing company {{ill|Hakubunkan|en|Hakubunkan|qid=Q11409000|lt=Hakubunkan|1=Hakubunkan}} takes its name from ''Hakubun'', an alternate pronunciation of Itō's {{ill|Given name|en|Given name|qid=Q202444|lt=Given name|1=Given name}}. ====Itō Hirobumi former residence in Hagi==== The house where Itō lived from age 14 in Hagi after his father was adopted by Itō Naoemon still exists, and is preserved as a museum. It is a one-story house with a thatched roof and a gabled roof, with a total floor area of 29 [[Pyeong#tsubo|''tsubo'']] and is located 150 meters south of the {{ill|Shōkasonjuku Academy|en|Shōkasonjuku Academy|qid=Q2326743|lt=Shōkasonjuku Academy|1=Shōkasonjuku Academy}}. The adjacent villa is a portion of a house built by Itō in 1907 in Oimura, Shimoebara-gun, Tokyo (currently [[:en:Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa, Tokyo]]). It was a large Western-style mansion, of which three structures, a part of the entrance, a large hall, and a detached room, were transported Hagi. The large hall has a mirrored ceiling and its wooden paneling uses 1000-year old cedar trees from {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|qid=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino, Nara}}.{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}} The buildings were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1932.{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217439|title=伊藤博文旧宅|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}} === In Korea === The [[:en:Annals of Joseon Dynasty|Annals of Sunjong]] record that {{ill|Gojong of Korea|en|Gojong of Korea|qid=Q257425|lt=Gojong|1=Gojong of Korea}} held a positive view of Itō's governorship. In an entry for 28 October 1909, almost three years after being forced to abdicate his throne, the former emperor praised Itō, who had died two days earlier, for his efforts to develop Japanese civilization in Korea. However, the integrity of Joseon {{transliteration|ja|silloks}} dated after the {{ill|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905|en|Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905|qid=Q233254|lt=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905|1=Japan–Korea Treaty of 1905}} is considered dubious by Korean scholars due to the influence exerted over record-keeping by the Japanese. Itō has been portrayed several times in {{ill|Cinema of Korea|en|Cinema of Korea|qid=Q483169|lt=Korean cinema|1=Cinema of Korea}}. His assassination was the subject of North Korea's ''An Jung-gun Shoots Itō Hirobumi'' in 1979 and South Korea's ''Thomas Ahn Joong Keun'' in 2004; both films made his assassin {{ill|An Jung-geun|en|An Jung-geun|qid=Q482413|lt=An Jung-geun|1=An Jung-geun}} the protagonist. The 1973 South Korean film ''{{ill|Femme Fatale: Bae Jeong-ja|en|Femme Fatale: Bae Jeong-ja|qid=Q5442908|lt=Femme Fatale: Bae Jeong-ja|1=Femme Fatale: Bae Jeong-ja}}'' is a biopic of Itō's alleged adopted Korean daughter [[Bae Jeong-ja]] (1870–1952). Itō argued the [[:en:Pan-Asian|Pan-Asian]] view that if East Asians did not co-operate closely with one another, Japan, Korea and China would all fall victim to Western imperialism. Initially, Gojong and the Joseon government shared that belief and agreed to collaborate with the Japanese military. Korean intellectuals had predicted that the victor of the Russo-Japanese War would assume hegemony over their peninsula, and as an Asian power, Japan enjoyed greater public support in Korea than Russia. However, policies such as land confiscation and the drafting of forced labor turned Korean popular opinion against the Japanese, a trend exacerbated by the arrest or execution of those who resisted.{{cite news|url=http://www.donga.com/docs/magazine/new_donga/200105/nd2001050030.html|script-title=ko:긴급대특집, 일본 역사교과서 왜곡파문|newspaper=[[Dong-a Ilbo|New DongA]]|author=Lee Jeong-sik (이정식)|date=May 2001|access-date=1 May 2012|language=ko|trans-title=Special report on Japan's history textbook issue.|quote=... initially many Koreans supported Japanese against Russians, and helped Japanese military. ... Many intellectuals had predicted that whoever wins the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, Joseon would be controlled by a victor. Still, they had hoped for the Asian power's victory. .... On 14 April 1904, Japan demanded unrestricted fishing rights all across Korean peninsular. On 28 June, Japan asked for the right to use every unclaimed land in Korea. Many Japanese gangsters had beaten Korean citizens in numerous occasions. ... —1904, U.S. diplomatic cable by Horace Allen, then U.S. representative in Korea. [...러·일전쟁 때 많은 조선인이 일본측에 동조했고, 일본군을 도왔다... 많은 지식인이 전쟁이 끝난 후에 조선은 승자에게 굴(屈)하고 주권을 상실할 것이라 예측했음에도, 러시아보다는 ‘동족(同族)’인 일본이 승리하기를 바랐다. ... (1) 1904년 4월14일. 일본은 조선반도 전역에서 거의 무제한적인 어업권을 요구했다. (2) 6월28일. 그들은 지금 조선 내 모든 황무지를 점거하고 사용할 수 있는 권리를 요구했다. (3) 많은 수의 일본인 불량배 노동자들이 조선 사람들을 괴롭히고 있다. ...''1904 년 주한미국공사 호레스 앨런의 보고서'']}} {{ill|An Jung-geun|en|An Jung-geun|qid=Q482413|lt=An Jung-geun|1=An Jung-geun}} was also a proponent of what was later called Pan-Asianism. He believed in a union of the three East Asian nations to repel Western imperialism and restore peace in the region. === Itō memorial temple built === On 26 October 1932, the Japanese unveiled in Seoul the Hakubun-ji [[:ja:博文寺|博文寺]] Buddhist Temple dedicated to Prince Itō. Full official name "Prince Itō Memorial Temple (伊藤公爵祈念寺院)". Situated in then Susumu Tadashidan Park on the north slope of Namsan, which after liberation became Jangchungdan Park 장충단 공원. From October 1945, the main hall served as student home, ca. 1960 replaced by a guest house of the Park Chung-Hee administration, then reconstructed and again a student guest house. In 1979 it was incorporated into the grounds of the Shilla Hotel then opened. Several other parts of the temple are still at the site. ==Genealogy== * '''Hayashi family'''
